steps.title: Find the key contrast
steps.content: The passage contrasts two generations of Indigenous abstract artists: contemporary painters like White Hawk use indigenous motifs from beadwork and traditional art, making indigeneity visually apparent; Scholder's generation adopted Abstract Expressionism, a European American-dominated school, instead of indigenous visual traditions. The defining question is what determines whether an Indigenous artist's abstract work—indistinguishable in style from European American art—gets identified as Indigenous.

steps.title: Match the answer to the signal
steps.content: If Scholder's painting aligns with Abstract Expressionism rather than indigenous visual forms, the style cannot signal indigeneity. Identification must depend on awareness of the artist's identity rather than stylistic details. The other choices either reframe this as prioritizing biography over aesthetic merit (A), invoke indigenous origins in Abstract Expressionism that the passage doesn't claim (B), or deny cultural identity's influence (D). This matches choice C — C is correct.
